messages.content: You are an expert EU proposal writer for IRIS Technology Solutions, a photonics and NIR spectroscopy company. Write in first-person plural (we/our). Use precise technical language. Follow EU Horizon proposal conventions: numbered tasks (**Task X.Y**), clear objectives, evidence-based claims. Describe communication activities, target audiences, key messages, and channels.
Write the Communication and open science section for a Horizon Europe proposal.

2.3 Summary  
SPECIFIC NEEDS  EXPECTED RESULTS  DEC MEASURES  
1. Elimination of toxic and carcinogenic Cr6+  
due to environmental & workers’ health  
issues from our PoP sector  
2. Replacement of Pd (CRM) from our plating  
industry  
3. Increase of adhesion of our PoP regardless of the  
substrate material  
4. Increase use of metal plated lightweight  
plastic components in our automotive and  
aerospace industry  
1. Development of a sprayable bio-based resin-based  
process for our PoP regardless of the plastic substrate  
2. Development of a Cr6+, Pd-free wet chemistry  
PoP process customized on plastic substrate  
3. A monitoring system based on spectroscopy  
principles, for inspection of surfaces at various  
steps of our FreeMe production process  
4. Development of multiscale models for materials  
modelling related to plastic surface treatment  
and adhesion enhancement  
5. A Decision support tool facilitating the adoption  
of the SSbD FreeMe technologies by our PoP  
industry.  

Exploitation activities:  
1. Dedicated business planning for our PoP to prepare industrial  
partners for reaching the market:  
2. Patent application for our two approaches  
3. IPR agreements inside and outside our consortium  
Dissemination activities:  
1. Scientific community: 12 Scientific publications, 25 oral  
presentations in conferences, 2 workshops and 2 trainings  
2. Industrial community: 6 participations in Fairs and  
partnering events, 6 Publications, 2 trainings  
3. Standardization, Regulatory bodies: PoliMi, CRF, IAI,  
Gaser and Cnano, active in liaison with these groups will  
interact for promotion of new technologies  
Communication Activities: Various events such as a shopping  
mall show, open labs (i.e. PoliMi, Gaser, Cnano), events  
organized in the premises of our end users  

TARGET GROUPS  OUTCOMES  IMPACTS  
Industry:  
1. Plating shops (contractors) active in our PoP  
currently utilizing Cr6+ processes  
2. In-house installation for our PoP  
3. Materials suppliers for our plating industry  
4. Manufacturers of plastic components  
5. OEMs in the field of automotive, aerospace,  
home appliances. In future applications  
fashion industry  
Scientific community: In the field of green  
chemistry and plating on plastic.  
Society: Workers in our PoP industry, citizens in  
the areas that our PoP facilities are located  

1. Gaser and Cnano apply the technologies  
(sprayable resin route and Cr6+, Pd-free wet  
route, respectively) to their premises  
2. CRF, IAI & ARC become the first adopters of  
our FreeMe technologies in their plated components  
3. Wide utilization of the DST web-platform from  
potential adopters of the technology, mainly  
plating on plastics shops and in-house facilities  
4. Enhance SSbD approaches based on the first  
industrial adoption of the technology  
Scientific: New breakthrough results on plating on polymeric  
substrates. Advancements in materials modelling.  
Economic: Secure supply chains by elimination of Pd use from  
our PoP.  
Technological: New improved processes and products in the  
growing markets of our PoP, automotive, aerospace and consumer  
goods. Widening the technology application to new markets  
such as fashion, electronics. Increase of coatings quality due to  
better inspection  
Societal: Significant reduction of Cr6+ environmental pollution,  
reduction of occupation cancer incidents and elimination in the  
future 2) Maintenance and/or creation of high tech jobs within  
EU  
Call: HORIZON-CL4-2021-RESILIENCE-01-11 — Safe- and sustainable-by-design polymeric materials

3.  Quality and efficiency of our implementation

You are an expert EU proposal writer for IRIS Technology Solutions, a photonics and NIR spectroscopy company. Write in first-person plural (we/our). Use precise technical language. Follow EU Horizon proposal conventions: numbered tasks (**Task X.Y**), clear objectives, evidence-based claims. Describe communication activities, target audiences, key messages, and channels.
Write the Communication and open science section for a Horizon Europe proposal.

2.3 Impact Summary  
KEY ELEMENT OF THE IMPACT SECTION  
SPECIFIC NEEDS  
•We need a sustainable production of bio-based materials that converts the classical lineal approach to a circular one.  
•We require adequate evaluation of the entire value chain to ensure environmentally friendly production from materials sourcing to End Of Life (recycle, reuse, refurbish).  
•We aim to create materials with bio and sustainable sources that possess better properties than conventional ones.  
•We will upgrade our manufacturing lines (retrofit and new disruptive equipment) to boost bio-based materials at the European level.  
•We will implement circular business practices to reduce environmental impacts, ensuring a sustainable economy, and including the reduction of waste in land and oceans.  
EXPECTED RESULTS  
•We will demonstrate the feasibility of manufacturing retrofit to overcome innovative bio-based production.  
•We will demonstrate disruptive innovation of bio-based products: bio-rubber, bioplastic, wood composites.  
•We will boost the efficiency and productivity of bio-based products by introducing ultrasound and microwave systems.  
•We will facilitate the commercialization of our products in the future by carrying out a circular and sustainability business assessment of value chains.  
•We will create 10-15 new jobs, of which 5 will be PhD positions (during our project).  

DISS. & EXPLOITATION & COMMUNICATION MEASURES  
Exploitation of the new product:  
•We will patent new manufacturing technologies (new tools, microwaves, and ultrasound systems).  
•We will launch new product lines.  
•We will propose further research around properties enhancement for different applications.  
•We will propose new standards to produce bio-based materials (bio content, GHG emissions, energy sources).  

Dissemination towards the scientific community and industry:  
•We will publish in open access journals and online repositories.  
•We will participate in conferences to disseminate our project outcomes.  
•We will create a GREEN-LOOP platform with social engagement and webinar sessions.  
•We will widely disseminate all public reports and publications, including on the horizon results platform, the innovation radar, and other EC publications.  
•We will hold a final event jointly with another EU project to disseminate key outcomes to stakeholders.  

Communication measures:  
We will run an awareness campaign to demonstrate the benefits of using bio-based materials against conventional ones, and circular approaches that can help the EU reach climate neutrality by 2050.  
TARGET GROUPS  
•We will target material producing industries, industry sectors such as construction, transportation, food and beverage, appliances and tools, agro-industry, building, and material logistics.  
•We will engage SMEs interested in adopting new technologies/knowledge.  
•We will reach out to the general public, citizens, and individuals interested in circular economy concepts and circular value chains.  
•We will connect with the scientific community, universities, research centers, and the private sector.  
•We will involve policymakers, regulatory bodies, standardization authorities, and environmental consultancies.  
•We will engage stakeholders from different value chains.  

OUTCOMES  
•We expect high use of the scientific discovery published in Open Science.  
•We aim for a better understanding by society of the role bio-based materials manufacturing can play in reaching climate neutrality by 2050.  
•After 5 years from the beginning of our project, we will start the commercialization, aiming to achieve a market share of 2% of bio-based materials in the long term.  
•We will exploit the final products in a circular business approach (LBRT, MYX, FHF, NCC, NIC, UBRIS).  
IMPACT  
•Social: i) We will reduce the ethical concerns related to the use of biomaterials fabricated from artificial substances including metals, polymers, and ceramics. ii) We will establish new rules and regulations for manufacturing processes. iii) We will create more than 415 jobs in the long term.  
•Environmental: i) We will increase the usability of materials and products after their useful life by including approaches for reuse, recycling, and refurbishing. ii) We will promote circular value chains to enhance the sources of raw materials and close loops. iii) Our retrofit and enhancement equipment will lead to a reduction of 20-30% in CO2 emissions.  
•Scientific: i) We will increase the development of bio-based materials; ii) We will create new knowledge in manufacturing tools and equipment; iii) We will generate know-how through our exploitation and dissemination plan.  

HORIZON-CL4-2021-TWIN-TRANSITION-01-05 Manufacturing technologies for bio-based materials  
Sustainable manufacture systems towards novel bio-based materials Page 29 of 45  

3. Quality and efficiency of the implementation

2.3 HW and SW integration and optimisation D2.4, D2.5 ■■ 3 Integration and trials in industry ML6, ML7 # # 3.1 Integration in industry D3.1 ■ 3.2 Processing trials and comparative testing D3.2, D3.3 ■ ■ 3.3 Demonstration to industry D3.4 ■ 4 Production ML8, ML10 # # 4.1 Certification and Testing D4.4 ■ 4.2 Design documentation package D4.1 ■ 4.3 Production Engineering D4.2 ■ 4.4 Production D4.3 ■ 5 Gear up to commercialisation ML9 # 5.1 Technology Watch and Knowledge Management D5.2 ■ 5.2 Business plan D5.3 ■ 5.3 The 4Ps Marketing Mix D5.1, D5.4 ■ ■ 6 Project Management 6.1 Monitoring and reporting 6.2 Quality and Risk Management 6.3 Innovation Management 6.4 Fulfilment of Contractual Requirements ● ■ ● ■ ● ■ D6.1, D6.2 ● ■ ● ■ ● ■ ● ■ ■ Deliverable / # Milestone / ◊ Project Kick Off Meeting ● Meetings of our Management Board ■ ■ ◊ ● ■ FIGURE 5- MANAGEMENT/ORGANISATIONAL STRUCTURE SMEINST-07-2016-2017 27 | P a g e Our Management Board, Chaired by General Manager, Mr. Colm Digby, will be responsible for the strategic management of our project in line with the business and commercial strategy of our company. Our Management Board will take all strategic decisions in relation to our project (e.g. decisions with regard to any major technical decisions, budgetary decisions, knowledge management/IP protection). Decisions will be taken following the normal decision-making procedures and voting rights of our Management Board. We will formally report the progress of our project to the Management Board every month and every quarter the budget spend will be reported to the board. Extraordinary meetings with the Management Board will be called as required to address, discuss and arrive at a swift resolution should any extraordinary problems or issues arise. Dr. Tim Kehoe, who is the Product Manager for our development, will lead the Product Development Team that will implement the work according to our project work plan. He will meet with the Project Manager and the development team on a weekly basis for steering technical discussions, monitoring the evolution of the work, measuring progress against our work plan, analysing potential risks and bottlenecks, contingency planning, etc. Issues that could have a strategic or critical impact on our project will be reported immediately to the General Manager, who will convoke an extraordinary meeting of the Management Board as appropriate. Oonagh Mc Nerney, the CEO, will lead the Commercialisation team, and she will be responsible for building the sales agent/distribution network internationally as well as further developing our business plan (with the inputs and approval of the Management Board). She will also coordinate with the marketing team to ensure our project dissemination and communication campaigns are aligned with the commercial strategy and she will lead our project’s engagement with our group of ‘beta clients’/early adopters. Together the Product Manager and the CEO will establish the Application Team that will provide technical support to the sales team. ■APPROPRIATENESS TO THE COMPLEXITY AND SCALE OF OUR PROJECT: We will manage the VISUM® project following the same structure and decision-making mechanisms as an internal product development project within VISUM, and this is considered as a highly appropriate approach. ■INNOVATION MANAGEMENT: Innovation Management is a function that exists within our organisation structure and is part of our ‘UNE-EN-ISO 166002: Management Systems for R&D&I’ certification. The structure consists of an Innovation Committee (comprised of the General Manager, Director, Deputy Director and the Commercial Director), and an Innovation Unit (which is led by the Innovation Unit Head- a role that is occupied by the Science and Technology Manager in VISUM, along with senior scientists, researchers and engineers from our company). The Innovation Committee (which is an advisory body to the Management Board) meets with the Innovation Unit on a monthly basis in order to track the innovation potential of all active projects, to analyse IP, to discuss technology watch, state-of-the-art, competition, and market considerations around the R&D lines that we are actively investing in. The Innovation Unit also assists the Innovation Committee in the development, maintenance and updating of the R&D Roadmap and the technology portfolio. The innovation management of VISUM will be performed by these Innovation Bodies within VISUM and will be managed through Task 6.3. ■CRITICAL RISKS: The critical risks (technology-, commercial-, and organisation-related) that could jeopardise our Innovation Project and appropriate risk-mitigation measures are provided in Table 3.2b. Through Task 6.2, a continual process of risk assessment and monitoring will ensure that the status of all risks is effectively managed and that action plans for all mitigation measures and contingency plans are implemented, monitored and reported. The process of risk identification and management will be based upon the principles and guidelines of the FERMA (ISO 31000 related) Standard 27. 3.3 CONSORTIUM AS A WHOLE (IF APPLICABLE) Not applicable as this is a single entity project. 3.4 RESOURCES TO BE COMMITTED TABLE 3.4A: SUMMARY OF STAFF EFFORT TABLE 3.4 B: ‘OTHER DIRECT COST’ ITEMS (TRAVEL, EQUIPMENT, INFRASTRUCTURE, GOODS AND SERVICES) 27 Federation of European Risk Management Associations ( www.ferma.eu ) 1 IRIS 18.0 96.0 29.0 29.0 22.0 14.0 208.0 18.0 96.0 29.0 29.0 22.0 14.0 208.0 Total WP6 Participant Short Name Participant No. Total Person/Months per participant WP1 WP4 WP5 WP2 WP3 SMEINST-07-2016-2017 28 | P a g e 4. MEMBERS OF OUR CONSORTIUM 4.1 PARTICIPANTS Participant no. 1 Name (short name)/Country VISUM Ltd. (VISUM)/Ireland Description of the legal entity Visum Ltd.- www.visum.ie (previously known as IRIS Advanced Engineering Limited) is a Dublin-based technology company located in the campus of University College Dublin. We were initially spun out as a branch facility of IRIS, S.L. from Spain (established in 2007 and with 53 staff) as part of a strategy to internationalize our company and establish an optical engineering division in Ireland. In 2013 this branch was converted into an indigenous Irish company. VISUM is an advanced engineering and R&D company, specializing in the field of process engineering. The facility in Dublin has its own team of optical, electronics and software engineers and has a fully equipped optics laboratory, mechanical and electronics workshop, and has solid expertise in developing spectroscopic and hyperspectral imaging solutions for industry (food, pharma and chemical advanced monitoring applications), as well as developing rigs for universities and research labs. We also have expertise in the development of cloud-enabled data management solutions for smart manufacturing and smart agriculture applications. VISUM is ISO 9001:2008 certified and also is certified under UNE-EN-ISO 166002: Management Systems R&D&i. Since 2011, VISUM has a Commercial Department with its own sales and marketing team. VISUM is a member of the following networks: SPIE (International Society for Optics and Photonics), SECPHO (Southern European Cluster of Photonics), SPIRE (Sustainable Process Industries). Main tasks VISUM Ltd. will fully implement Phase 2 of our innovation project as the sole applicants. We have the technical and operational capacity and staff, as well as the research, engineering and product development capability, market knowledge and B2B commercial expertise that can be dedicated to this development. We will also expand our team in Dublin through new dedicated hires. How the legal entity profile matches the tasks in the proposal VISUM offers advanced monitoring and control turnkey solutions based on: - Chemical monitoring (NIR)- handheld device and in-line solutions - Hyperspectral imaging (foreign body detection and chemical composition analysis) - Industrial cloud solutions and data management solutions for data processing, decision support, automation and data analysis (big production data) We are engaged in R&D activities in the fields of optical and spectroscopy technologies (e.g. MEMs spectrophotometers, fluorescence and Raman) for process monitoring for on-line and PAT applications in food, pharmaceuticals and the process industries, as well as for controlling intensified processes. We have successfully developed pre-competitive IR spectroscopy prototypes in several FP7 projects (whereby, we have been responsible for integrating the sensors within the equipment and developing the optomechanics, in addition to the chemometric models for data processing, and the ad hoc software for supervision and control): VITISPEC –FTIR-ATR for monitoring wine composition-, OPTIMALT –NIR for real-time determination of barley protein and moisture in a harvester-, OPTIJECT –FT-NIR for monitoring plastic injection-, SPECTRAFISH –hyperspectral imager for fish QC-, OPTICLEAN –hyperspectral imager for CIP validation-, THIME –ellipsometer and hyperspectral imager for monitoring thin-film optoelectronic devices production lines. We have successfully completed Phase 1 SME Instrument VISUM (Grant Agreement- 684767), where we: • Applied for a registered trademark for the VISUM brand

3. Quality and efficiency of the implementation  
3.1 Work plan and 
resources  
3.1.1. Overall structure 
of our work plan  
Our RETAIN work plan is composed of 8 work packages. WP1-4 focus on specific aspects of our RETAIN recycling chain, being logistics, repair/reuse/remanufacture, deconfectioning, recycling, and conversion of recyclates into value-added products. WP5 is the actual demonstration part of our project, whereas WP6-7 are supporting tasks. WP8 finally covers our project management and coordination of RETAIN.  
WP1 (M1-M24) focuses on logistics, traceability, monitoring, and sorting. We, as lead partner IRIS, along with partner VEOLIA and several other partners, will develop and implement a strict gate control procedure that distinguishes between various tarp qualities. The development and integration of a digital product passport for tarp will be an important part of our gate control procedure. WP2 (M1-M30) deals with repair, reuse, and remanufacturing, and deconfectioning of post-production and EOL tarps. We, as lead partner VWERVEN and partner EKOTREND, will develop a (semi)automized deconfectioning system to remove eyelets, buckets, zippers, and other functionalities from EOL tarps. We, along with partners SIOEN, SF, and several other partners, will study repair/reuse/remanufacturing practices to increase the lifetime of tarps. In WP3 (M1-M30), we will study the actual recycling of tarps. Various mechanical recycling (EKOTREND, KKF, VWERVEN) and solvent-based recycling (SF and CTB) methodologies will be investigated and prepared for demonstration in WP5. Furthermore, we will develop selective extraction of valuable additives from tarps (AIMPLAS) and strategies definition and development of a decision support tool for recycling processes (RINA-C). Various other partners will be active in this WP under the leadership of partner CTB. WP4 (M1-M39) covers the conversion of recycled and biobased materials into high-value products, producing new tarps through extrusion coating/calendaring as executed by lead partner SIOEN and partner MEHLER. Various other partners such as RENO, BIG, SATTLER, and SF will apply plasticizers, PVC, and other recyclates into their products. Finally, we, as partner CTB and some other partners, will focus on standardization aspects related to tarp recycling. In WP5 (M19-M42), we will execute the actual demonstration of circularity for complex PVC-based value chains in three EU regions. We, as lead partner VEOLIA, will coordinate this work. This WP covers 3 main activities: demonstration of the gate control process, demonstration of recycling processes and technologies, and uptake of recycled products. In the final stage of our project, we will assess the RETAIN recycling chain and the demos to conclude on the technical and economic aspects of the RETAIN recycling chain. WP6 (M19-M42), led by partner RINA-C, will focus on sustainability assessment studies. In WP7 (M1-M42), we, as lead partner EuPC and all other partners, will execute all activities related to market acceptance, uptake, and deployment. This WP includes dissemination, communication, exploitation, and training & learning activities. Finally, in WP8 (M1-M42), all our project management and coordination activities will take place.  

 
 
3.1.2. Timing of the Work Packages and main tasks - Gantt Chart  
  
 
MS4 - GO for screening demo activities  
MS5 - GO for recycling demo activities, relying on the developed options for remanufacturing, deconfectioning, recycling, and ecodesign  
MS6 - End of experimental demo activities, switch focus on evaluation and finalization of documentation  

 
 
MS7 - Ready for ‘Handover’, i.e. to start the beyond RETAIN phase

BBI JU specific questions on our project’s implementation RIA projects: 1) Lab and industrial scale implementation 1. Has an optimized and stable lab scale unit, or unit in the industrially relevant environment been achieved? How many tests have we run so far (for each developed method, if applicable)? The production of prototypes was made by TEXOL, BIOINICIA and FIBROLINE plants. Prototypes validated in a relevant industrial environment were achieved in our POLYBIOSKIN project. The description of the different properties of prototypes is listed in a previous table. Biocompatibility with skin and biodegradability was tested for all the prototypes. Moreover, specific tests, in agreement with the Regulatory Contest, were carried out for comparing the new products with the current commercial alternative. 2. If applicable, how is our project progressing towards a DEMO plant for the developed products/materials/building blocks/etc…? Some more investments are necessary to progress to a DEMO scale in diaper, beauty mask and wound dressing. For this reason, the possibility of applying for further funding opportunities was Page 116 2) Innovation and Impact 3. Is any IP applied in our project? a. Is it new IP or have we obtained access to existing IP? b. If we have access to existing IP, how did we obtain the IP (IP of project beneficiaries, licences,…)? c. If we use new IP, how many patents have been submitted at the time of reporting? The production of the CN-LN complexes were protected by two patents licensed by TEXOL. A patent related to the DBD-plasma application for surface decontamination is under assessment at IRIS. The novelty has been fully described in D9.7 4. Has any M.Sc. or Ph.D. thesis been submitted to the relevant academic institutions? Our POLYBIOSKIN consortium involves several academic groups involved in didactical activity. The general technical and scientific topics of our POLYBIOSKIN project were cited in lectures related to different topics: • In courses for Master Students related to “Processing and Recycling of Polymers”, where our POLYBIOSKIN project is cited as an example of industrial use of the flat die extrusion for obtaining top-sheets for sanitary and biodegradable polymers (PLA and PHA based) to promote their organic and mechanical recyclability. In fact, their composting is quite easy, but their recycling can also be possible and at the end, when, after several cycles the properties will not allow a further recycling, the anaerobic digestion/composting will be anyway possible. Hence promoting the selective collection of biobased materials is a mandatory step for demonstrating its effective beneficial effect on the environment. The current contest is in fact characterized by the selective collection of fossil plastics that lead to their recycling or energy recovery. The transition to biobased plastics implies that biodegradable and nonbiodegradable polymers should be distinguished and recycling and energy recovery will be adopted for nonbiodegradable materials, whereas recycling and anaerobic digestion/composting will be adopted for the biodegradable ones. ● EU should promote a good dissemination strategy regarding projects, involving on one side market stakeholders to explain biodegradable products to prevent leakages from waste streams, leading to ecologically and economically less favourable disposal routes. In order to activate more forces along the common End of Life practices, it is suggested to policymakers to keep activating the research and education sector: ● EU, as well as national and regional institutions should promote research projects such as our POLYBIOSKIN and activities in companies that can unlock the tremendous opportunity that exists beyond packaging applications (which have been the target of many initiatives to date) to substitute conventional fossil-based materials with more sustainable bio-based ones that can also achieve improved functionalities in demanding high value market niches, such as our important targets of diapers, personal care and cosmetic products. The work that we will perform in relation to the development of specific technologies and concepts needed to realise the value chains, proving the principles in pilot installations, validating the manufactured prototypes, as well as performing communication activities to help to increase consumer acceptance, contributing to concerted policy and labelling, and heightening overall awareness of bio-based products as well as recycling and reuse, will all have an important contribution to the future market exploitation of project results and improved market penetration of biobased products. ● EU should gather the results of different projects to promote End of Life practices involving Institutions (from EU up to single municipalities of Member States) and decide regarding the possibility of selecting post-consumer materials based on bio-based and biodegradable polymers (PLA and PHA based) to promote their organic and mechanical recyclability. You are an expert EU proposal writer for IRIS Technology Solutions, a photonics and NIR spectroscopy company. Write in first-person plural (we/our). Use precise technical language. Follow EU Horizon proposal conventions: numbered tasks (**Task X.Y**), clear objectives, evidence-based claims. Describe communication activities, target audiences, key messages, and channels.
Write the Communication and open science section for a Horizon Europe proposal.

at IRT in the field of network technologies, since our graduation in 2008. We studied media technology at the University of Applied Science in Dusseldorf with a focus on media informatics. Our main research area is on video encoding and streaming in order to ensure a wide-ranging reception of media content for the general public in the sense of the German speaking public broadcasters. Between 2013 and 2016, we led IRT’s work within the EU project TV-RING. We joined IRT in 2012, focusing on the media streaming solutions for different platforms like Web, mobile devices or HbbTV and application development. We received our BEng. Degree in Audiovisual Media from the Hochschule der Medien Stuttgart. During our time at IRT, we contributed to different collaborative research projects such as "Alert4All", "TV-Ring" or "Hbb4All". Currently, we lead "Argos", a Big Data driven research project that enables broadcasters to monitor their video and audio delivery over the internet. We work at IRT in various European research projects like P2P-Next, kuMobile, Savant and Sambits, as well as, national and regional research projects like WiMAC@home or MifoP (Multimedia follows person) and ADAMs (Adaptive Multimodal streaming). We completed our studies in Electrical Engineering and Information Technology at Munich University of Applied Sciences (FH) in 2000. We participate in international standardisation groups of EBU, DVB, DLNA and DASH. Since 2015, we are conducting MPEG-DASH Seminars at IRT. We are working with the public broadcasters of ARD and ZDF for online distribution on PC, HbbTV and mobile devices over the open Internet. Our working areas are interoperability and perceptual quality of audio and video-encoding as well as quality of experience in Adaptive Streaming systems for HbbTV, PC and mobile devices. We support ARD, ZDF, CE-manufacturers and encoder-vendors in testing of AV-profiles with the focus of multiplatform distribution using MPEG-DASH for Live and OnDemand over CDN infrastructures. We are IRT’s DPO. We studied Media Informatics at the University of Applied Sciences in Dusseldorf, Germany. We received our diploma in 2011 after having completed our thesis on “The comparison of adaptive streaming technologies” at the IRT. After our studies, we joined IRT as a Scientific Engineer. Among other things, we are currently focusing on the development of backend applications and involved in the EU H2020 project ‘HRadio’. Since 2016, we are responsible for all data protection questions concerning GDPR. We will serve as IRT’s data privacy single point of contact in 5G-SOLUTIONS. We are the Head of IRT’s radio systems department. We completed our Masters in Radio Communications at the Technical University of Ulaanbaatar in Mongolia 1997, where we graduated with first class honours. In 1998, we continued our studies in the area of telecommunications at the Technical University Darmstadt in Germany, where we were awarded a Doctoral Degree in Electrical Engineering in 2010. After our doctoral studies, we gained research experience at the University of Applied Sciences in Munich, where we developed technology for maintaining high reliability of public announcement systems. In 2012, we joined IRT as a Senior Researcher working on Local Spectrum Portfolio Management and in particular, on frequency planning and web graphical tool of booking for PMSE and C-PMSE systems. Our main research interests lie in several fields of radio communications, including propagation, mobile radio system architecture, digital television, spectrum efficiency, frequency management and wireless microphones. We are now active in software defined radio implementation of LTE Transmitter Physical Layer according to 3GPP Release 14. We are IRT’s DPO. We studied Media Informatics at the University of Applied Sciences in Dusseldorf, Germany. We received our diploma in 2011 after having completed our thesis on “The comparison of adaptive streaming technologies” at the IRT. After our studies, we joined IRT as a Scientific Engineer. Among other things, we are currently focusing on the development of backend applications and involved in the EU H2020 project ‘HRadio’. Since 2016, we are responsible for all data protection questions concerning GDPR. We will serve as IRT’s data privacy single point of contact in 5G-SOLUTIONS. We are the Head of IRT’s radio systems department. We completed our Masters in Radio Communications at the Technical University of Ulaanbaatar in Mongolia 1997, where we graduated with first class honours. In 1998, we continued our studies in the area of telecommunications at the Technical University Darmstadt in Germany, where we were awarded a Doctoral Degree in Electrical Engineering in 2010. After our doctoral studies, we gained research experience at the University of Applied Sciences in Munich, where we developed technology for maintaining high reliability of public announcement systems. In 2012, we joined IRT as a Senior Researcher working on Local Spectrum Portfolio Management and living lab case studies in terms of organisation, planning, and set up of the core communication network platforms in each test site for optimising the existing communication network as appropriate (and any plant conditioning that this may entail), and building the interfaces between transceivers and sensors for wireless communication. We will also integrate our hyperspectral imaging device to our PATBox platform for processing the data obtained from the analyser and using a 5G testbed as the communication interface with the cloud. Cloud-based supercomputing will allow us to use updated machine learning algorithms for improving the current capabilities –mainly based on rather simple chemometric tools. It ultimately implies more comprehensive (accurate) identification and quantification to take artificial vision to a wider paradigm: chemical vision. We will lead the Industry 4.0 field trials, overseeing the setup of the test environment performing the relevant planning and LL operational management for the Factories of the Future Living Lab. We will also supervise the conducting of advanced field trials in the Factories of the Future to validate the technological and business performance of 5G. Therefore, we will lead the following tasks: 
- T4.1 LL planning, setup & operational management 
- T4.2 LL1 execution: Factories of the Future 
- T4.3 Performance evaluation and lessons learned 
- T7.2 MLL execution: Concurrent validation of eMBB, mMTC & URLLC service classes from LL1-LL4

We are Project and Innovation Managers at IRIS. We obtained our BSc degree in Telecommunication Engineering from the University of Pisa (Italy) and the MSc degree in Telecommunication Engineering at University of Pisa (Italy) and Polytechnic University of Catalunya (Spain), where we performed a Master in Computer Science and Networking with Sant’ Anna School of Advanced Studies. We were also visiting students at University of San Diego (USD), California, USA. We have expertise in Networking Architecture, Software Defined Networking (SDN), Wireless Communication and Transmission Systems. We spent more than 2 years as R&D Engineers and Technical Project Managers in the Mobile Wireless Internet department of i2CAT Research Center, Barcelona (Spain). During that period, we worked on several projects on the development of the 5G architecture, radio access and backhaul network, and SDN paradigms. We are authors of a scientific publication of an SDN-based system with joint access-backhaul and multi-tenant capabilities on small cells. Before our experience in R&D, we worked in the B2B environment managing

Metric parameter B1: Personnel cost People are the biggest cost in most enterprises, therefore, with these services can we do more with the same resources? can we do more with less resources? are more expensive resources required? which are more specialised and hard to find? B2: Overhead cost How can our new service reduce non-people costs? energy costs? green: reduce CO2? less infrastructure, less buildings, less transport? B3: Time to market How does 5G accelerate the provision of new innovative services to our customers / citizens and which of them are critical for our business (competitive differentiation) and for public services (deliver return-on-taxes to citizens)? B4: Safety How does 5G improve our confidence in technology acceleration with respect to the safety of our people, safety of our processes, safety of our ecosystem? B5: Data privacy How does 5G improve our confidence in the protection of the individual in the digital age of their data and their identity? B6: Accessibility How does 5G help a wider community to share in the benefits of an enhanced digital age: more accessible in cost? types of location? device types? disabilities? KPI How to achieve the validation of our 5G-PPP contractual business KPIs BA1 This KPI will be measured and monitored by gathering the published public figures from annual reports for worldwide R&D investment in the ICT sector, and subsequently discerning the proportion of the R&D ICT expenditure related to 5G. The substantiation of this value will benefit from a poll/questionnaire amongst industry stakeholders, such as 5G network vendors, mobile network operators, and UE manufacturers. Investment in 5G, with respect to the total telecom R&D budget, will gradually increase with time as 5G moves into full standardisation, development and production over the next 2-3 years. In order to identify an investment value specific for Europe, the poll/questionnaire will be sent to a representative set of key 5G EU stakeholders (e.g. stakeholders from our 5G-PPP projects incl. 5G-SOLUTIONS consortium partners). BA2 This KPI will be measured by taking into consideration the budget allocated to SMEs from their participation in all our 5G-PPP projects with respect to the total funded budget. BA3 Our project will span over 3 years and it is estimated to be completed by the summer 2022, at which stage there will be representative data to enable its validation. These measurements will again be taken from a poll/questionnaire amongst the same industry stakeholders as in BA1. Living Lab 1: Factories of the Future (FoF) Part Role IRIS LL leader. We will provide support to all factory sites in the UCs in terms of organisation, planning, and set up of the core communication network platforms at each site for optimising the existing communication network as appropriate, and building the interfaces between transceivers and sensors for wireless communication. We will also leverage our expertise in Process Analytical Technology solutions for real-time process monitoring and control within UC1.1 in PBGS, whereby our Multispectral Vision system will be implemented for processing the data obtained from the analysers, and using the 5G nodes as the communication interface with the cloud. Cloud-based supercomputing will be used to enable updated machine learning algorithms to be used for improving the control within UC1.1 in PBGS, whereby our Multispectral Vision system will be implemented for processing the data obtained from the analysers, and using the 5G nodes as the communication interface with the cloud. Cloud-based supercomputing will be used to enable updated machine learning algorithms to be used for improving the current capabilities, which are predominantly based on artificial intelligence and chemometric tools. GLAN Participation in UC1.2, based on the maintenance function within GLAN in their dairy and nutritional ingredients processing factory plant. Typically, key assets and equipment have been maintained using scheduled and usage-based maintenance strategies. Predictive maintenance uses key data feeds and agents to constantly monitor condition feeds and recognise patterns leading to equipment failure allowing for timely and effective intervention. The key benefit is that assets can be sweated for longer and avoid unplanned outages. NTNU YARA In collaboration with TNOR, we will equip the NTNU Manulab Robotics and Industry 4.0 laboratories with a 5G infrastructure for ultra-low latency closed-loop control of moving and stationary robots. In particular, we will be involved in UC1.1, UC1.2, UC1.3 and primarily in UC1.5, which will monitor new robots in a factory for remote operation by real-time audio/video-feeds requiring high data rates. YARA will support us in the execution of the field trials in UC1.5. PBGS We will participate in UC1.1, to enable full automation of a digital plant, leveraging advanced technology, such as visual recognition of quality control on, for instance, products on a line, multispectral and hyperspectral imaging of complex products. The data needs to be collected and optimised across the plants. For UC1.4 home services will be validated for laundry that require connected machines to continuously track and anticipate consumer laundry (sensors, imaging & spectral data). OMES For UC1.2, we will enhance the operation of our Smart Manufacturing Execution Systems & Performance Management Systems, whereby the OPS Smart Manufacturing datahub – a centralised Hub for connectivity & data storage (for data input and extraction from various devices across the plant) – will be used for 5G validation to ensure efficiency, speed, capacity, latency, reliability, accuracy and timeliness in data input. Efficiency, Intelligence and Data Analysis within the FoF is dependent on the future-proofing of communications to this Hub via 5G TNOR UOP UOP will support the execution of field trials in UC1.1-UC1.4 by installing private 5G nodes in PBGS and GLAN factories. TNOR will support and timeliness in data input. Efficiency, Intelligence and Data Analysis within the FoF is dependent on the future-proofing of communications to this Hub via 5G TNOR UOP UOP will support the execution of field trials in UC1.1-UC1.4 by installing private 5G nodes in PBGS and GLAN factories. TNOR will support the execution of field trials in UC1.5 through the 5G-VINNI Norway 5G facility. Test Objectives Requirements Key Actors To test the stringent requirements for indoor mission–critical process optimisations in a heterogeneous environment. To test ultra-low latencies (<1ms) in densities of up to 1 transceiver/sensor per m2 To test the network’s reliability of at least 99.999% Complementary communication channels for accessing remote services based on high speed broadband connection Real data and the respective data sources Installation of 5G RAN node in PGBS facility IRIS PGBS Test Objectives Requirements Key Actors To test inside our factory, the coverage, coexistence and interference management of 5G wireless technologies, such as 3G, 4G Wi-Fi, Bluetooth. To test the reliability (99.99%) of the wireless data transfer from the sensors. Complementary communication channels for accessing remote services based on high speed links. Real data and respective data sources (both fixed or mobile) Installation of a private 5G node in GLAN facility IRIS GLAN OMES Test Objectives Requirements Key Actors To test the availability/ coverage of the network for immediate use To test the security of the network to protect against non-authorised access and malicious takeover of a machine or a plant Installation of private 5G R
